name: Braun SSA Construction
summary: Matthias Braun et al. (CC 2013): build SSA on the fly directly from a linear instruction stream, without computing dominance information upfront. Per-block per-variable current-definition map plus incomplete φ-functions for early reads. Used by Cranelift, Firm/libFirm, and several JIT backends.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Matthias Braun et al. (CC 2013) presented an algorithm that builds SSA on the fly, directly from a linear instruction stream, without computing dominance information upfront. The core idea is a per-block, per-variable *current definition* map plus **incomplete φ-functions** for variables read in a block before all its predecessors have been sealed. When a block is later sealed (all predecessors known), incomplete φ-functions are filled in and possibly optimised away.

The algorithm is dramatically simpler than Cytron's — a few hundred lines in practice — and crucially it plays well with JITs that build IR as they parse bytecode. Produces minimal SSA for reducible CFGs and near-minimal SSA for irreducible ones.

sources.label: Simple and Efficient Construction of SSA Form (Braun et al., CC 2013)

sections.title: Mechanism
sections.content:
For each variable in each block, maintain a *current definition* map. On a read, look up the current definition; if absent, recursively look up in predecessors. If predecessors are not all sealed yet (incomplete CFG), insert an *incomplete φ-function* — a placeholder that the algorithm will fill in once predecessors are known.

When a block becomes *sealed* (all predecessors are known), every incomplete φ in that block is filled by reading the variable in each predecessor. The algorithm then attempts to optimise the φ: if all incoming values are the same (after the recursive read), the φ is trivial and can be replaced with that value; this may trigger further optimisation in users.

The entire algorithm is local: it does not need a dominator tree or dominance-frontier computation. The trade-off is that block sealing must be tracked carefully, and the resulting SSA is minimal only for reducible CFGs (irreducible CFGs may produce slightly more φ-functions than necessary).

sections.title: Production usage
sections.content:
**Cranelift**, **Firm/libFirm**, and several recent JIT backends use Braun's algorithm directly. The lesson generalises: for any compiler that constructs IR from a linear input (bytecode, source tokens) rather than from a complete pre-built CFG, Braun's algorithm avoids the front-end-heavy step of building dominance information just to construct SSA.

For a batch compiler with a stable CFG already in hand, Cytron's algorithm is fine and the tooling literature is richer. Both approaches are correct; the choice is dictated by when the CFG becomes complete relative to when SSA construction needs to run.

sources.label: Braun et al. — Simple and Efficient SSA Construction
